rows.answer: Fixed in `packages/zod/src/v4/core/util.ts`: `merge()` now throws `.merge() cannot be used on object schemas containing refinements` when either operand has checks (refinements), matching the behavior already established for `.pick()`, `.omit()`, and `.partial()`. Verified with a manual repro and confirmed the existing object/pick/omit/partial test suites (166 tests) still pass.

All 3688 tests pass. Fix confirmed and complete.

## Summary

Root cause: `merge()` in `packages/zod/src/v4/core/util.ts` unconditionally set `checks: []`, silently discarding refinements from both operands â€” unlike `.pick()`/`.omit()`/`.partial()`, which throw when the source schema has checks.

Fix (`packages/zod/src/v4/core/util.ts:687`): added a guard before merging that throws `.merge() cannot be used on object schemas containing refinements` if either operand (`a` or `b`) has non-empty `checks`, mirroring the existing pattern in `pick`/`omit`/`partial`.

Verified with the issue's PoC (now throws instead of silently dropping the refinement), confirmed refinements on either side of `.merge()` trigger the error, confirmed normal merges without refinements still work, and ran the full zod test suite (3688 tests, all passing).

The fix works end-to-end â€” the generated JSON schema `pattern` now matches all example CIDRs, and both the pattern and Zod's runtime validation agree.

## Summary

**Root cause:** `regexes.cidrv6` in `packages/zod/src/v4/core/regexes.ts` used a hand-written pattern (`([0-9a-fA-F]{1,4})?::(...)`) that only allowed 0â€“1 groups before `::`, unlike `regexes.ipv6` which correctly enumerates all valid compressed forms.

**Fix:** Rebuilt `cidrv6` from the existing (correct) `ipv6` regex plus the CIDR prefix suffix, instead of a separately-maintained pattern:

```ts
export const cidrv6: RegExp = new RegExp(`^${ipv6.source.slice(1, -1)}\\/(12[0-8]|1[01][0-9]|[1-9]?[0-9])$`);
```

This eliminates the divergence between runtime validation and the JSON-schema-exported pattern, and avoids duplicating the complex IPv6 alternation logic in two places.

Verified: full `zod` test suite (336 files, 3808 tests) passes, and manual checks confirm `2001:db8::/32`, `2001:db8:1::/48`, `2001:0db8:85a3::/64`, `fe80::/10`, and `::/0` all now match the generated JSON schema pattern.

Fixed `regexes.cidrv6` in `packages/zod/src/v4/core/regexes.ts` to enumerate all valid IPv6 compression forms (mirroring how `regexes.ipv6` handles it) instead of the incomplete `([0-9a-fA-F]{1,4})?::(...)` alternative that only allowed 0â€“1 groups before `::`.

Verified: all cases from the bug report (`2001:db8::/32`, `2001:db8:1::/48`, `2001:0db8:85a3::/64`, `fe80::/10`, `::/0`) now match both at runtime and via the generated JSON schema `pattern`, invalid CIDRs are still rejected, and the full zod test suite (3808 tests) passes.

## Summary

**Root cause:** `util.shallowClone` (`packages/zod/src/v4/core/util.ts:399`) is the function `.default()` uses to avoid returning the same object reference on every parse. It already special-cased plain objects (`{...o}`) and arrays (`[...o]`), but fell through to returning the original reference `o` for everything else â€” including `Map`, `Set`, and `Date`. Since `$ZodDefault`'s `defaultValue` getter (`v4/core/api.ts:1461`) calls `shallowClone` on each access, a `Map`/`Set` default was handed back unchanged and identical across parses, so mutating one parsed result mutated all future ones (matching the reported `itemsMap.set(...)` leaking into the next parse).

**Fix:** extended `shallowClone` to clone `Map`, `Set`, and `Date` via their copy constructors, consistent with the existing object/array handling.

Verified with the reporter's exact repro (now prints `0`/`0` instead of `0`/`1`) and the full `zod` test suite (326 files, 3680 tests, all passing, including the existing `"defaulted object/array schema returns shallow clone"` tests in `default.test.ts` that establish this is the intended contract).

Working tree is clean apart from the fix, and the repro scratch files were removed.

## Root cause

In `packages/zod/src/v4/core/schemas.ts`, both `$ZodUnion` and `$ZodXor` had this pattern in their init function:

```js
const single = def.options.length === 1;
const first = def.options[0]._zod.run;   // executed unconditionally
```

`def.options[0]` is only guaranteed to exist when `single` is true, but the line ran regardless of `single`. For `z.union([])`/`z.xor([])`, `def.options` is empty, so `def.options[0]` is `undefined`, and `.â€‹_zod` throws `Cannot read properties of undefined (reading '_zod')` â€” an internal `TypeError`, not a `ZodError`.

The rest of the parse logic (`handleUnionResults`/`handleExclusiveUnionResults`) already handles zero options correctly â€” with no successful/nonaborted results it falls through to push an `invalid_union` issue â€” so no input can ever satisfy an empty union, matching the `never` type semantics the reporter expected.

## Fix

Made `first` lazy, only computed when `single` is actually true:

```js
const first = single ? def.options[0]._zod.run : undefined;
...
if (single) {
  return first!(payload, ctx);
}
```

Applied identically to both `$ZodUnion` and `$ZodXor`. `ZodMiniUnion`/mini's xor reuse this same core init, so they're fixed too. v3's `ZodUnion._parse` already used `.map()` over options and didn't have this bug.

Verified `z.union([])` and `z.xor([])` now construct fine and `.safeParse()` returns a proper `invalid_union` `ZodError` instead of throwing. Full test suite (3696 tests) passes.

All v3 and v4 tests pass (the one datetime flake is unrelated and passes in isolation).

## Root cause

`z.toJSONSchema()` attaches a non-enumerable `~standard` property (with function values) to its output object (`to-json-schema.ts`), by design, since PR #5477 added Standard Schema support so the JSON Schema result can itself be re-validated/queried via `~standard.jsonSchema.input/output`. That part is intentional and has passing tests that depend on it (`result["~standard"].jsonSchema.input(...)`, etc.), so it can't simply be removed.

The actual bug is in `$ZodRecord`'s generic (non-enum/literal key) parse branch in `packages/zod/src/v4/core/schemas.ts`, which iterated `Reflect.ownKeys(input)` â€” this returns **all** own keys, including non-enumerable ones. That's inconsistent with the sibling branch just above it (fixed enum/literal keys), which uses `for...in` (enumerable-only). Since `z.json()` is built from `record(string(), jsonSchema)`, it walked into the hidden `~standard` property, tried to validate its function values as JSON, and failed.

## Fix

Changed the generic record branch to only enumerate **enumerable** own keys (`Object.keys` + enumerable symbols), matching plain-object/JSON semantics and the sibling branch's behavior. This makes `record()`/`z.json()` correctly ignore non-enumerable properties like `~standard` while leaving direct property access (`result["~standard"]`) fully intact.

## Root cause

`z.toJSONSchema()` intentionally attaches a non-enumerable `~standard` property to its output (to let the JSON Schema itself act as a Standard Schema with `.jsonSchema.input/output` methods â€” see `packages/zod/src/v4/core/to-json-schema.ts:500`). That's a deliberate, tested feature (`packages/zod/src/v4/classic/tests/standard-schema.test.ts:110`), not something to remove.

The actual bug is in `$ZodRecord`'s generic key-validation branch (`packages/zod/src/v4/core/schemas.ts:2818`), used by `z.json()`'s `record(string(), jsonSchema)` arm. It iterated with `Reflect.ownKeys(input)`, which â€” unlike `Object.keys`/`for...in` (used everywhere else, e.g. `$ZodObject`'s catchall handling) â€” includes **non-enumerable** own properties. So it picked up the hidden `~standard` key, tried to validate its value (an object containing functions) against the record's value schema, and failed.

## Fix

In `packages/zod/src/v4/core/schemas.ts`, skip non-enumerable own keys in the record's generic-key iteration, matching the enumerable-only semantics used by object schemas and plain JS iteration (`for...in`, `Object.keys`, `JSON.stringify`), while still supporting symbol keys via `Reflect.ownKeys`:

```ts
for (const key of Reflect.ownKeys(input)) {
  if (key === "__proto__") continue;
  if (!Object.prototype.propertyIsEnumerable.call(input, key)) continue;
  ...
```

## Verification

- Repro matching the issue (`~standard` present + non-enumerable, `z.json().safeParse(jsonSchema).success`) now passes.
- Full suite: `packages/zod` â€” 3589/3590 tests pass; the one failure (`datetime.test.ts` "redos checker", a 5s timeout) is a pre-existing flake reproduced identically on `main` without my change, unrelated to this fix.

## Root cause

In `packages/zod/src/v4/core/schemas.ts`, `$ZodRecord`'s parser has two code paths:

1. **Finite key set** (`def.keyType._zod.values` is defined â€” used for `z.literal()`, `z.enum()`, unions of literals, etc.): iterates the known valid key literals, looks up `input[key]`, runs the *value* schema, but **never runs the key schema** â€” it just copies the raw literal `key` straight into the output object.
2. **Generic key** (no finite value set): iterates `Reflect.ownKeys(input)`, runs `def.keyType._zod.run(...)` on each key, and correctly uses the *transformed* result as the output property name.

`z.literal("a").transform(() => "b")` is a `$ZodPipe`, whose `_zod.values` getter is `def.in._zod.values` â€” i.e. it forwards the **input** side's literal set (`{"a"}`), so record hits path 1. Since path 1 never invokes the key schema, the transform is silently skipped and the original key `"a"` is kept â€” even though the TS types (`core.output<Key>`) correctly show the output type as transformed, since they're derived independently.

## Fix

Made path 1 also run `def.keyType._zod.run(...)` on each candidate key and use `keyResult.value` as the output property name (mirroring path 2's behavior), pushing any key-schema issues via `util.prefixIssues`.

Verified with a standalone repro (`z.record(z.literal("a").transform(() => "b"), z.string()).parse({a: "hello"})` now returns `{b: "hello"}`), and ran the full test suite: 330 files / 3710 tests pass, including type-checking, with no regressions.

Clean diff, only the intended fix remains.

## Root cause

`z.record()`'s parser (`packages/zod/src/v4/core/schemas.ts`) has a fast path used when the key schema exposes a discrete set of `values` (literals, enums, or a `.transform()`/pipe wrapping one â€” since `$ZodPipe`/`$ZodCodec` inherit `values` from their *input* schema, e.g. the `"a"` literal, not the transformed `"b"` output).

In that fast path, the code iterated the known input `values`, ran only `valueType` on `input[key]`, and wrote the result back using the **untransformed** `key` directly (`payload.value[key] = result.value`), never invoking `def.keyType._zod.run(...)` on the key itself. So any `.transform()` on the key schema was silently skipped â€” the general (non-fast-path) branch a few lines below already did this correctly, which is why only the enumerated-values case was affected.

## Fix

In the fast path, run the key through `def.keyType._zod.run(...)` to get the transformed key, and use `keyResult.value` as the output property instead of the raw `key` (matching the pattern already used by the general path, including the existing "async key schemas unsupported" guard).

Verified with a standalone repro (`z.record(z.literal("a").transform(() => "b"), z.string())` now correctly produces `{ b: "hello" }`) and the full test suite (330 files / 3710 tests, all passing, no regressions).

## Summary

Root cause: `floatSafeRemainder` (in `packages/zod/src/v4/core/util.ts`, mirrored in `packages/zod/src/v3/types.ts`) counted decimal places by splitting `toString()` on `"."`. For numbers in scientific notation with a fractional mantissa (e.g. `(2.5e-7).toString() === "2.5e-7"`), this only counted the mantissa's fractional digits ("5e-7", length 4) and ignored the exponent entirely, undercounting the true decimal-place count (8). The existing scientific-notation handling only covered the `step` parameter, and even then only for integer mantissas (`1e-7`), not fractional ones (`2.5e-7`).

Fix: replaced the ad-hoc split/regex logic with a `decimalPlaceCount` helper that parses scientific notation generally â€” extracting both the mantissa's fractional digit count and the exponent, and combining them (`mantissaDecCount - exponent`, clamped at 0) â€” then applies this helper uniformly to both `val` and `step` in both the v4 (`util.ts`) and legacy v3 (`types.ts`) implementations.

Verified:
- `z.number().multipleOf(1e-7).safeParse(2.5e-7)` and `3.3e-7` now correctly fail; `3e-7` still passes.
- Existing number/bigint test suites (v3 and v4) pass with no regressions.
